bug_report.Title: xarray.concat fails when input Datasets have different variable sets (does not ignore missing variables / fill with NA)
bug_report.Description: xarray.concat currently requires that all input Datasets share the same set of variables (and consistent coordinate/data-var roles). When some Datasets lack variables present in others, concat raises an error and forces callers to pre-create dummy NaN variables or drop mismatched variables. Users expect an option (pandas-like outer behavior) to take the union of variables and fill missing values with NA instead.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) xarray/core/concat.py::_dataset_concat — Strict presence assumption for variables_to_merge: _dataset_concat constructs `variables_to_merge = (coord_names | data_names) - concat_over - dim_names` and then, for each dataset, computes `absent_merge_vars = variables_to_merge - set(ds.variables)` and raises ValueError when this set is non-empty (lines 314–320). This encodes a strict 'variable must be present in every input' assumption for merged (non-concatenated) variables.

2) xarray/core/concat.py::_dataset_concat (concatenation loop) — Strict presence assumption for concat_over variables: when building the per-dataset list to concatenate, the code uses `[ds.variables[k] for ds in datasets]` inside a call to ensure_common_dims and catches KeyError to raise ValueError("%r is not present in all datasets.") (lines 359–365). This enforces that every variable the code will concatenate must be present in every dataset.

3) xarray/core/merge.py::unique_variable and xarray/core/variable.py::concat (callee expectations) — API contract: unique_variable(name, variables, ...) and Variable.concat (and IndexVariable.concat) expect to receive a full list of Variable objects (one per input dataset) with compatible dims. unique_variable uses `variables[0]` and compares/combines actual Variable objects (lines ~80–142 in merge.py). Variable.concat requires consistent dims among the supplied Variable objects (raises "inconsistent dimensions" when they differ; lines ~1552–1555 in variable.py). IndexVariable.concat requires all inputs to be IndexVariable objects (raises TypeError otherwise; lines ~2002–2006 in variable.py). Because _dataset_concat raises on missing variables before synthesizing anything, these callee functions do not receive placeholder Variables and therefore cannot themselves produce NA-filled results for missing inputs.

4) Additional strict-presence checks sharing the same root cause pattern — coordinate vs data-variable role mismatches and final coordinate presence check in _dataset_concat: _dataset_concat raises when a name is a coordinate in some datasets but a data variable in others (`both_data_and_coords` check, lines 289–293), and it checks after building result_vars that no coordinate names were dropped (`absent_coord_names = coord_names - set(result.variables)` and raise if non-empty, lines 370–375). These are the same strict 'must be present / consistent role' assumptions applied to coordinates.

5) Diagnostic relationships / placeholder creation formula (values discovered during investigation): to treat a missing variable as present, a synthesized placeholder Variable must match the variable's dims and per-dataset shapes and use appropriate dtype/fill semantics. Concretely:
   - Source of per-dataset sizes: use dims_sizes (from _parse_datasets) for non-concatenation dims and concat_dim_lengths (from _calc_concat_over) for the concat dimension. ensure_common_dims uses these to set shapes (see ensure_common_dims using `tuple(dims_sizes.get(d, dim_len) for d in common_dims)` at lines 351–355).
   - Fill/dtype selection: if fill_value is the sentinel dtypes.NA, obtain (dtype, fill_value) via dtypes.maybe_promote(sample.dtype) (xarray/core/dtypes.py lines 45–78). If fill_value is explicit, compute the compatible dtype via dtypes.result_type(sample.dtype, np.asarray(fill_value)) (xarray/core/dtypes.py lines 145–169) and use the explicit fill_value for np.full. The placeholder's data can be constructed as `np.full(shape_tuple, fill_value, dtype=dtype)` and wrapped as Variable(sample.dims, data).
   - Note on IndexVariable: IndexVariable.concat requires all inputs be IndexVariable objects; creating a plain Variable placeholder for missing IndexVariable inputs will route concat to Variable.concat (losing IndexVariable-specific behavior). This is a related behavioral consequence to consider when synthesizing placeholders (see IndexVariable.concat TypeError check at variable.py lines ~2002–2006).

unstacked: Dataset

        Examples
        --------
        >>> import xarray as xr
        >>> arr = xr.DataArray(
        ...     np.arange(6).reshape(2, 3),
        ...     coords=[("x", ["a", "b"]), ("y", [0, 1, 2])],
        ... )
        >>> data = xr.Dataset({"a": arr, "b": arr.isel(y=0)})
        >>> data
        <xarray.Dataset>
        Dimensions:  (x: 2, y: 3)
        Coordinates:
          * x        (x) <U1 'a' 'b'
          * y        (y) int64 0 1 2
        Data variables:
            a        (x, y) int64 0 1 2 3 4 5
            b        (x) int64 0 3
        >>> stacked = data.to_stacked_array("z", ["y"])
        >>> stacked.indexes["z"]
        MultiIndex(levels=[['a', 'b'], [0, 1, 2]],
                labels=[[0, 0, 0, 1], [0, 1, 2, -1]],
                names=['variable', 'y'])
        >>> roundtripped = stacked.to_unstacked_dataset(dim="z")
        >>> data.identical(roundtripped)
        True

        See Also
        --------
        Dataset.to_stacked_array
        """

        idx = self.indexes[dim]
        if not isinstance(idx, pd.MultiIndex):
            raise ValueError(f"'{dim}' is not a stacked coordinate")

        level_number = idx._get_level_number(level)
        variables = idx.levels[level_number]
        variable_dim = idx.names[level_number]

        # pull variables out of datarray
        data_dict = {}
        for k in variables:
            data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

        # unstacked dataset
        return Dataset(data_dict)

    def transpose(self, *dims: Hashable, transpose_coords: bool = True) -> "DataArray":
        """Return a new DataArray object with transposed dimensions.

        Parameters
        ----------
        *dims : hashable, optional
            By default, reverse the dimensions. Otherwise, reorder the
            dimensions to this order.
        transpose_coords : boolean, default True
            If True, also transpose the coordinates of this DataArray.

        Returns
        -------
        transposed : DataArray
            The returned DataArray's array is transposed.

        Notes
        -----
        This operation returns a view of this array's data. It is
        lazy for dask-backed DataArrays but not for numpy-backed DataArrays

from typing import (
    TYPE_CHECKING,
    AbstractSet,
    Any,
    Dict,
    Hashable,
    Iterable,
    List,
    Mapping,
    NamedTuple,
    Optional,
    Sequence,
    Set,
    Tuple,
    Union,
)

import pandas as pd

from . import dtypes, pdcompat
from .alignment import deep_align
from .duck_array_ops import lazy_array_equiv
from .utils import Frozen, compat_dict_union, dict_equiv
from .variable import Variable, as_variable, assert_unique_multiindex_level_names

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from .coordinates import Coordinates
    from .dataarray import DataArray
    from .dataset import Dataset

    DimsLike = Union[Hashable, Sequence[Hashable]]
    ArrayLike = Any
    VariableLike = Union[
        ArrayLike,
        Tuple[DimsLike, ArrayLike],
        Tuple[DimsLike, ArrayLike, Mapping],
        Tuple[DimsLike, ArrayLike, Mapping, Mapping],
    ]
    XarrayValue = Union[DataArray, Variable, VariableLike]
    DatasetLike = Union[Dataset, Mapping[Hashable, XarrayValue]]
    CoercibleValue = Union[XarrayValue, pd.Series, pd.DataFrame]
    CoercibleMapping = Union[Dataset, Mapping[Hashable, CoercibleValue]]


PANDAS_TYPES = (pd.Series, pd.DataFrame, pdcompat.Panel)

_VALID_COMPAT = Frozen(
    {
        "identical": 0,
        "equals": 1,
        "broadcast_equals": 2,
        "minimal": 3,
        "no_conflicts": 4,
        "override": 5,
    }
)


def broadcast_dimension_size(variables: List[Variable],) -> Dict[Hashable, int]:
    """Extract dimension sizes from a dictionary of variables.

    Raises ValueError if any dimensions have different sizes.
    """
    dims: Dict[Hashable, int] = {}
    for var in variables:
        for dim, size in zip(var.dims, var.shape):
            if dim in dims and size != dims[dim]:
                raise ValueError("index %r not aligned" % dim)
            dims[dim] = size
    return dims


class MergeError(ValueError):
    """Error class for merge failures due to incompatible arguments.
    """

    # inherits from ValueError for backward compatibility
    # TODO: move this to an xarray.exceptions module?


def unique_variable(
    name: Hashable,
    variables: List[Variable],
    compat: str = "broadcast_equals",
    equals: bool = None,
) -> Variable:
    """Return the unique variable from a list of variables or raise MergeError.

    Parameters
    ----------
    name : hashable
        Name for this variable.
    variables : list of xarray.Variable
        List of Variable objects, all of which go by the same name in different
        inputs.
    compat : {'identical', 'equals', 'broadcast_equals', 'no_conflicts', 'override'}, optional
        Type of equality check to use.
    equals: None or bool,
        corresponding to result of compat test

    Returns
    -------
    Variable to use in the result.

    Raises
    ------
    MergeError: if any of the variables are not equal.
    """
    out = variables[0]

    if len(variables) == 1 or compat == "override":
        return out

    combine_method = None

    if compat == "minimal":
        compat = "broadcast_equals"

    if compat == "broadcast_equals":
        dim_lengths = broadcast_dimension_size(variables)
        out = out.set_dims(dim_lengths)

    if compat == "no_conflicts":
        combine_method = "fillna"

    if equals is None:
        # first check without comparing values i.e. no computes
        for var in variables[1:]:
            equals = getattr(out, compat)(var, equiv=lazy_array_equiv)
            if equals is not True:
                break

        if equals is None:
            # now compare values with minimum number of computes
            out = out.compute()
            for var in variables[1:]:
                equals = getattr(out, compat)(var)
                if not equals:
                    break

    if not equals:
        raise MergeError(
            f"conflicting values for variable {name!r} on objects to be combined. "
            "You can skip this check by specifying compat='override'."
        )

    if combine_method:
        for var in variables[1:]:
            out = getattr(out, combine_method)(var)

    return out


def _assert_compat_valid(compat):
    if compat not in _VALID_COMPAT:
        raise ValueError(
            "compat={!r} invalid: must be {}".format(compat, set(_VALID_COMPAT))
        )


MergeElement = Tuple[Variable, Optional[pd.Index]]


def merge_collected(
    grouped: Dict[Hashable, List[MergeElement]],
    prioritized: Mapping[Hashable, MergeElement] = None,
    compat: str = "minimal",
) -> Tuple[Dict[Hashable, Variable], Dict[Hashable, pd.Index]]:
    """Merge dicts of variables, while resolving conflicts appropriately.

    Parameters
    ----------

        Type of equality check to use when checking for conflicts.

    Returns
    -------
    Dict with keys taken by the union of keys on list_of_mappings,
    and Variable values corresponding to those that should be found on the
    merged result.
    """
    if prioritized is None:
        prioritized = {}

    _assert_compat_valid(compat)

    merged_vars: Dict[Hashable, Variable] = {}
    merged_indexes: Dict[Hashable, pd.Index] = {}

    for name, elements_list in grouped.items():
        if name in prioritized:
            variable, index = prioritized[name]
            merged_vars[name] = variable
            if index is not None:
                merged_indexes[name] = index
        else:
            indexed_elements = [
                (variable, index)
                for variable, index in elements_list
                if index is not None
            ]

            if indexed_elements:
                # TODO(shoyer): consider adjusting this logic. Are we really
                # OK throwing away variable without an index in favor of
                # indexed variables, without even checking if values match?
                variable, index = indexed_elements[0]
                for _, other_index in indexed_elements[1:]:
                    if not index.equals(other_index):
                        raise MergeError(
                            "conflicting values for index %r on objects to be "
                            "combined:\nfirst value: %r\nsecond value: %r"
                            % (name, index, other_index)
                        )
                if compat == "identical":
                    for other_variable, _ in indexed_elements[1:]:
                        if not dict_equiv(variable.attrs, other_variable.attrs):
                            raise MergeError(
                                "conflicting attribute values on combined "
                                "variable %r:\nfirst value: %r\nsecond value: %r"

        shortcut : bool, optional

            (2) The action of `func` creates arrays with homogeneous metadata,

            speedup. This should be the case for many common groupby operations

_ROLLING_REDUCE_DOCSTRING_TEMPLATE = """\
Reduce this object's data windows by applying `{name}` along its dimension.

Parameters
----------
**kwargs : dict
    Additional keyword arguments passed on to `{name}`.

class Rolling:
    """A object that implements the moving window pattern.

    See Also
    --------
    Dataset.groupby
    DataArray.groupby
    Dataset.rolling
    DataArray.rolling
    """

    __slots__ = ("obj", "window", "min_periods", "center", "dim", "keep_attrs")
    _attributes = ("window", "min_periods", "center", "dim", "keep_attrs")

    def __init__(self, obj, windows, min_periods=None, center=False, keep_attrs=None):
        """
        Moving window object.

        Parameters
        ----------
        obj : Dataset or DataArray
            Object to window.
        windows : A mapping from a dimension name to window size
            dim : str
                Name of the dimension to create the rolling iterator
                along (e.g., `time`).
            window : int
                Size of the moving window.
        min_periods : int, default None
            Minimum number of observations in window required to have a value
            (otherwise result is NA). The default, None, is equivalent to
            setting min_periods equal to the size of the window.
        center : boolean, default False
            Set the labels at the center of the window.
        keep_attrs : bool, optional
            If True, the object's attributes (`attrs`) will be copied from
            the original object to the new one.  If False (default), the new
            object will be returned without attributes.

        Returns
        -------
        rolling : type of input argument
        """
        if len(windows) != 1:
            raise ValueError("exactly one dim/window should be provided")

        dim, window = next(iter(windows.items()))

        if window <= 0:
            raise ValueError("window must be > 0")

        self.obj = obj

        # attributes
        self.window = window
        if min_periods is not None and min_periods <= 0:
            raise ValueError("min_periods must be greater than zero or None")
        self.min_periods = min_periods

        self.center = center
        self.dim = dim

        if keep_attrs is None:
            keep_attrs = _get_keep_attrs(default=False)
        self.keep_attrs = keep_attrs

    @property
    def _min_periods(self):
        return self.min_periods if self.min_periods is not None else self.window

    def __repr__(self):
        """provide a nice str repr of our rolling object"""

        attrs = [
            "{k}->{v}".format(k=k, v=getattr(self, k))
            for k in self._attributes
            if getattr(self, k, None) is not None
        ]
        return "{klass} [{attrs}]".format(
            klass=self.__class__.__name__, attrs=",".join(attrs)
        )

    def __len__(self):
        return self.obj.sizes[self.dim]

    def _reduce_method(name: str) -> Callable:  # type: ignore
        array_agg_func = getattr(duck_array_ops, name)
        bottleneck_move_func = getattr(bottleneck, "move_" + name, None)

        def method(self, **kwargs):
            return self._numpy_or_bottleneck_reduce(
                array_agg_func, bottleneck_move_func, **kwargs
            )

        method.__name__ = name
        method.__doc__ = _ROLLING_REDUCE_DOCSTRING_TEMPLATE.format(name=name)
        return method

    argmax = _reduce_method("argmax")
    argmin = _reduce_method("argmin")
    max = _reduce_method("max")
    min = _reduce_method("min")
    mean = _reduce_method("mean")
    prod = _reduce_method("prod")
    sum = _reduce_method("sum")
    std = _reduce_method("std")
    var = _reduce_method("var")
    median = _reduce_method("median")

    def count(self):
        rolling_count = self._counts()
        enough_periods = rolling_count >= self._min_periods
        return rolling_count.where(enough_periods)

    count.__doc__ = _ROLLING_REDUCE_DOCSTRING_TEMPLATE.format(name="count")

        obj : DataArray

            dim : str
                Name of the dimension to create the rolling iterator
                along (e.g., `time`).
            window : int
                Size of the moving window.
        min_periods : int, default None
            Minimum number of observations in window required to have a value
            (otherwise result is NA). The default, None, is equivalent to
            setting min_periods equal to the size of the window.
        center : boolean, default False
            Set the labels at the center of the window.
        keep_attrs : bool, optional
            If True, the object's attributes (`attrs`) will be copied from
            the original object to the new one.  If False (default), the new
            object will be returned without attributes.

        rolling : type of input argument

        --------
        DataArray.rolling
        DataArray.groupby

        if keep_attrs is None:
            keep_attrs = _get_keep_attrs(default=False)
        super().__init__(
            obj, windows, min_periods=min_periods, center=center, keep_attrs=keep_attrs

    def __iter__(self):
        stops = np.arange(1, len(self.window_labels) + 1)
        starts = stops - int(self.window)
        starts[: int(self.window)] = 0
        for (label, start, stop) in zip(self.window_labels, starts, stops):
            window = self.obj.isel(**{self.dim: slice(start, stop)})

            counts = window.count(dim=self.dim)
            window = window.where(counts >= self._min_periods)

            yield (label, window)

    def construct(self, window_dim, stride=1, fill_value=dtypes.NA):

        fill_value: optional. Default dtypes.NA
            Filling value to match the dimension size.

        Returns
        -------
        DataArray that is a view of the original array. The returned array is
        not writeable.

        Examples
        --------
        >>> da = xr.DataArray(np.arange(8).reshape(2, 4), dims=("a", "b"))

        >>> rolling = da.rolling(b=3)

        <xarray.DataArray (a: 2, b: 4, window_dim: 3)>
        array([[[np.nan, np.nan, 0], [np.nan, 0, 1], [0, 1, 2], [1, 2, 3]],
               [[np.nan, np.nan, 4], [np.nan, 4, 5], [4, 5, 6], [5, 6, 7]]])
        Dimensions without coordinates: a, b, window_dim

        >>> rolling = da.rolling(b=3, center=True)
        >>> rolling.construct("window_dim")
        <xarray.DataArray (a: 2, b: 4, window_dim: 3)>
        array([[[np.nan, 0, 1], [0, 1, 2], [1, 2, 3], [2, 3, np.nan]],
               [[np.nan, 4, 5], [4, 5, 6], [5, 6, 7], [6, 7, np.nan]]])
        Dimensions without coordinates: a, b, window_dim

        """

        from .dataarray import DataArray

        window = self.obj.variable.rolling_window(
            self.dim, self.window, window_dim, self.center, fill_value=fill_value
        )
        result = DataArray(
            window, dims=self.obj.dims + (window_dim,), coords=self.obj.coords

        return result.isel(**{self.dim: slice(None, None, stride)})

Thought: From DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset we saw that it selects along the variable_dim and squeezes. The reported MergeError arises when Dataset(data_dict) tries to construct a Dataset from multiple DataArrays with conflicting coordinate 'y' values. Specifically, when variables are single-dim (only x), the assigned coordinate for 'y' during to_stacked_array was probably None (NaN) for that level, and to_unstacked_dataset will sel(variable_dim=k).squeeze(drop=True). For single-dim variable, selecting along variable_dim on the MultiIndex with None labels may produce a DataArray with coordinate 'y' equal to <some> that conflicts across variables.

I will inspect tests mentioned earlier: xarray/tests/test_dataset.py test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset to see expected behavior and how it handles 1D variables.

Statement of the bug
- DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset builds data_dict by selecting and squeezing each variable after selecting the requested MultiIndex level, then calls Dataset(data_dict). For stacked coordinates where the MultiIndex had only a single level (i.e., the stacked dimension itself is just the variable level), the selected DataArrays still carry the stacked coordinate (the MultiIndex coordinate) as a coordinate named dim. When Dataset(data_dict) is created, merge_data_and_coords attempts to merge those coordinates (the stacked dim coordinate) across all DataArrays. Because the per-variable selected DataArrays have incompatible values for that coordinate (they are slices of the stacked MultiIndex), merge complains and raises MergeError (“conflicting values for variable 'y' ...”). This happens specifically for the single-dimension-stacked case (the user’s MCVE), but can also occur with other shapes when the stacked coordinate persists as a coordinate inside each extracted DataArray.

Primary code responsible
- DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (xarray/core/dataarray.py: lines ~1953–1967). It fails to remove the stacked MultiIndex coordinate from the selected per-variable DataArrays before handing them to Dataset(...), thereby allowing a coordinate with name dim to conflict across variables during the Dataset construction.

Checkpoint 4 — Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (concise)
- Modify DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset so that after selecting and squeezing each per-variable DataArray, we drop the stacked coordinate (the coordinate named dim) from that DataArray before putting it into the data_dict. Concretely, change:

    for k in variables:
        data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

  to:

    for k in variables:
        da = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)
        # remove the stacked MultiIndex coordinate to avoid coordinate conflicts
        # when constructing the unstacked Dataset
        if dim in da.coords:
            da = da.drop_vars(dim, errors="ignore")
        data_dict[k] = da

- This removes the stacked MultiIndex coordinate from each selected DataArray, so Dataset(data_dict) will not attempt to merge conflicting 'dim' coordinates.

Patch location
- xarray/core/dataarray.py: method DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (currently lines ~1953–1967). Only this method needs to change.

Why this fixes the bug
- The MergeError occurs because the selected DataArrays contain a coordinate named after the stacked dimension (a MultiIndex) whose underlying Index objects differ between per-variable slices. Dropping that stacked coordinate before constructing a Dataset avoids the merge-time conflict. The intended semantics of to_unstacked_dataset is to expand a MultiIndex level to dataset variables and keep remaining levels as coordinates/dimensions; keeping the original stacked MultiIndex coordinate (the entire dimensional MultiIndex) in each variable is unnecessary and causes the merging problem. Dropping the stacked coordinate is correct and consistent with the intended result.

Now answer all required challenge questions referencing inspected code:

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- The current code assumes that it's safe to hand per-variable DataArrays (resulting from sel(...).squeeze()) to Dataset(...) without removing the stacked MultiIndex coordinate 'dim'. My fix changes that assumption: it asserts that the stacked coordinate 'dim' should not remain as a coordinate on the per-variable DataArrays when re-constructing the unstacked Dataset.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- There are no numeric hardcoded constants relevant to this assumption in the files we inspected. The issue is a logical one (presence of the coordinate named dim in da.coords) rather than a numeric assumption. The relevant hardcoded strings/identifiers are:
  - The packed usage of dim and variable_dim names when creating/using the MultiIndex in Dataset.to_stacked_array (e.g., new_dim name, variable_dim default "variable").
  - In Dataset.to_stacked_array ensure_stackable, assign_coords[dim] = None is used to set missing dims -> this causes NaN positions in the MultiIndex for missing levels. These are not numeric constants but play into how the stacked MultiIndex is formed. If we remove the stacked MultiIndex from the per-variable DataArrays on unstacking, these are no longer problematic.

Assess each possible hardcoded value from component classes:
- Dataset.to_stacked_array uses variable_dim="variable" by default. This is not changed; the fix only uses the provided dim argument to drop the stacked coordinate.
- None/NaN as placeholder for missing coordinates used by ensure_stackable is unchanged. Dropping the stacked coordinate makes the subsequent Dataset construction ignore those placeholders.

Conclusion: No numeric hardcoded constants must be made dynamic for this fix.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse operation is Dataset.to_stacked_array -> DataArray (stacked), and DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset should be its inverse.
- Dataset.to_stacked_array builds the stacked MultiIndex and attaches it as a coordinate named new_dim (data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx)). The reverse operation currently (before fix) calls sel and squeeze and returns Dataset(...) of the extracted arrays, but because the stacked coordinate remains attached it causes merges to fail.
- My proposed fix removes the stacked MultiIndex coordinate 'dim' from each per-variable DataArray so Dataset(data_dict) receives clean DataArrays with the expected original coordinates and no conflicting new_dim coordinate. That restores the roundtrip:

Concrete substitution / trace for the MCVE:

- Given original:
    arr = DataArray(np.arange(3), coords=[('x', [0,1,2])])  # dims ('x',)
    data = Dataset({'a': arr, 'b': arr})
    stacked = data.to_stacked_array('y', sample_dims=['x'])  # stacked dims: ('x','y') where y is MultiIndex over variable
- stacked.indexes['y'] is a pd.MultiIndex with levels[0]=['a','b'], names[0]='variable'.
- Call stacked.to_unstacked_dataset('y'):
  - idx = self.indexes['y'] (MultiIndex)
  - level_number = idx._get_level_number(level=0) -> 0
  - variables = idx.levels[0] -> Index(['a','b'])
  - variable_dim = idx.names[0] -> 'variable'
  - For k in ['a','b']:
    - self.sel({'variable':k}) selects the subarray along the 'variable' level -> returns DataArray with a y-dimension length 1 (the selected MultiIndex entry)
    - .squeeze(drop=True) removes the length 1 y-dimension -> returns DataArray dims ('x',) but coords likely still include the original stacked coordinate 'y' as a coordinate (IndexVariable) with different internal index objects per selection.
    - Dataset({...}) tries to merge those coords and finds conflicting values for 'y' -> MergeError.
- After fix:
  - After squeeze, drop the coordinate named 'y' (the stacked coordinate). The per-variable DataArray has only the original coords (x). Dataset(data_dict) now sees two DataArrays with same x coordinate, no conflicting 'y' coordinate -> merge succeeds and roundtrip returns expected Dataset.

Do we need to change Dataset.to_stacked_array or other methods?
- No. Dataset.to_stacked_array already constructs the stacked MultiIndex in the stacked DataArray. The correct inverse is that unstacking returns a Dataset with variables corresponding to MultiIndex level entries and without the stacked MultiIndex coordinate attached to each variable. Dropping the stacked coordinate on the unstack side is the correct approach.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset is changed so the per-variable DataArray has the stacked coordinate removed if present. This should not break other DataArray methods because:
  - DataArray.drop_vars exists and returns DataArray; using errors='ignore' avoids errors if the stack coordinate is not present.
  - Other methods that rely on the stacked coordinate being present only operate on stacked DataArray objects; after to_unstacked_dataset has created a Dataset, it returns out of DataArray context. We are not changing other methods’ behavior.
- Verify methods that call to_unstacked_dataset:
  - Tests in test_dataset.py call .to_unstacked_dataset and expect roundtrip to Dataset. The test test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset and test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset_different_dimension should pass with the fix (the MCVE that failed will now succeed).
- Other code using sel(...).squeeze(...).drop_vars(...) is safe.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are necessary. The fix uses existing DataArray APIs:
  - sel, squeeze, drop_vars (which calls into Dataset.drop_vars via _to_temp_dataset/_from_temp_dataset).
- No parent method needs overriding. Specifically, parent class methods that perform complementary operations (like DataArray._to_temp_dataset and Dataset.__init__ merging) continue to work; we just ensure the inputs to Dataset.__init__ are clean (i.e., no conflicting stacked coordinate).

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (complete analysis and fix)

Root cause
- DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset collected per-variable DataArrays by selecting the requested MultiIndex level and squeezing out the stacked dimension, but it left the original stacked coordinate (the MultiIndex coordinate with name dim) attached to each per-variable DataArray. When these per-variable DataArrays are passed to Dataset(data_dict) for construction, Dataset.__init__ attempts to merge coordinates across inputs, and finds conflicting values for the stacked coordinate (different Index objects), raising MergeError. This is especially exposed when variables have only a single non-sample dimension (the stacked MultiIndex contains only one level), as in the user’s MCVE.

Every location that needs to change
- Single change required:
  - xarray/core/dataarray.py: DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset

What the current code does and what assumption it encodes
- Current code (excerpt):

    idx = self.indexes[dim]
    ...
    variables = idx.levels[level_number]
    variable_dim = idx.names[level_number]

    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

    return Dataset(data_dict)

- Assumption encoded (implicit): after sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True) the resulting DataArray can be used directly as a value in the Dataset constructor, i.e., it has coordinates that will merge cleanly across variables. That assumption fails because the stacked coordinate (dim) is left as a coordinate in each DataArray, and these differ between variables.

What the fix should be (concrete)
- Remove the stacked MultiIndex coordinate (the coordinate named dim) from each selected DataArray before placing it into data_dict. Use DataArray.drop_vars(..., errors="ignore") for safety.

Exact patch (conceptual diff)

In xarray/core/dataarray.py, in DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset:

Replace:

    # pull variables out of datarray
    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

    # unstacked dataset
    return Dataset(data_dict)

With:

    # pull variables out of datarray
    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        da = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)
        # drop the stacked coordinate (the MultiIndex for `dim`) from each
        # selected DataArray before constructing a Dataset to avoid merge
        # conflicts on that coordinate.
        # errors='ignore' used so we do nothing if the coordinate is absent.
        da = da.drop_vars(dim, errors="ignore")
        data_dict[k] = da

    # unstacked dataset
    return Dataset(data_dict)

Notes
- Using errors="ignore" ensures that if the coordinate is not present (for some reason), no error is raised.
- We only remove the coordinate named dim, which is the stacked coordinate name, not any of the other level coordinates that should remain in the unstacked variables (e.g., other MultiIndex levels are typically promoted to coordinates/dimensions by Dataset.unstack or other code paths).

Any other locations to change?
- I reviewed Dataset.to_stacked_array and Dataset.unstack and merge.py; they do not require changes. They produce a MultiIndex coordinate and the unstack path needs to remove the stacked MultiIndex when expanding to variables. No further code modifications are required.

Why this is a complete fix
- The MergeError arises during Dataset construction when merging the stacked coordinate across the per-variable DataArrays; removing that coordinate removes the source of conflict. This addresses the failing MCVE and maintains the expected roundtrip behavior for stack/unstacking. The fix does not break other behaviors because:
  - It only drops the stacked coordinate on the per-variable DataArrays being put into the Dataset (the stacked MultiIndex is still present for the stacked DataArray until to_unstacked_dataset is called).
  - Existing tests that exercise to_stacked_array -> to_unstacked_dataset roundtrip (including different-dimension variables) remain valid and should pass.

Suggested test to add / update
- Add a test reproducing the MCVE to ensure the roundtrip works for single-dimension variables:
  - The test in xarray/tests/test_dataset.py already has similar cases, but ensure a case like:

    def test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset_single_dim():
        arr = xr.DataArray(np.arange(3), coords=[("x", [0, 1, 2])])
        data = xr.Dataset({"a": arr, "b": arr})
        stacked = data.to_stacked_array("y", sample_dims=["x"])
        roundtripped = stacked.to_unstacked_dataset("y")
        assert_identical(data, roundtripped)

- This will capture the previously failing MCVE.

Summary of the fix
- Single-line logic change: when constructing the dict of variables in DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset, drop the stacked coordinate dim from each selected DataArray before passing them to Dataset(). This prevents MergeError conflicts on the stacked coordinate and restores the expected roundtrip behavior.

- Prepare the exact patch (git diff) for xarray/core/dataarray.py.
- Run through the unit tests that exercise stack/unstack and to_stacked_array/to_unstacked_dataset to confirm the change (I can't run tests here, but I can indicate the tests that should be run).

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness analysis

I examined the draft enhanced report and verified each diagnostic claim against the code. Below I summarize what I checked, why, and what I found (quoting or referencing source lines where appropriate). I also checked for any missing secondary findings and for impact completeness.

1) Methods cited in the draft — verification

- DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset
  - Why I checked: the draft attributes the failure primarily to this method.
  - What I fetched: xarray/core/dataarray.py (DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset).
  - Source (relevant excerpt, lines shown):
    - xarray/core/dataarray.py lines 1953–1967:
      - idx = self.indexes[dim]
      - level_number = idx._get_level_number(level)
      - variables = idx.levels[level_number]
      - variable_dim = idx.names[level_number]
      - data_dict = {}
      - for k in variables:
          data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)
      - return Dataset(data_dict)
  - Verification: the draft claim that DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset selects per-variable slices with sel(...).squeeze(drop=True) and then hands them directly to Dataset(...) is accurate; the code does exactly that (lines 1961–1967). The method does not explicitly remove or alter the stacked coordinate `dim`.

- Dataset.to_stacked_array
  - Why I checked: the draft says the stacked MultiIndex coordinate is created here and attached to the stacked DataArray.
  - What I fetched: xarray/core/dataset.py (Dataset.to_stacked_array).
  - Source (relevant excerpt):
    - The function builds stackable variants (ensure_stackable) and concatenates them (lines ~3454–3473).
    - It then coerces MultiIndex levels and assigns the MultiIndex as a coordinate:
      - idx = data_array.indexes[new_dim] (line ~3478)
      - new_idx = idx.set_levels(levels) (line ~3482)
      - data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx) (line 3483)
  - Verification: the draft claim that Dataset.to_stacked_array creates the stacked MultiIndex and assigns it as an IndexVariable on the data array is accurate and supported by the above lines. The stacked coordinate therefore exists as a named coordinate (the `new_dim`) on the stacked DataArray.

- Merge/merging logic that raises MergeError
  - Why I checked: the draft links the MergeError message to Dataset construction merging coordinates.
  - What I fetched: xarray/core/merge.py
  - Source (relevant excerpts):
    - unique_variable raises MergeError when variables do not match: lines 141–145.
    - merge_collected raises MergeError when index objects for the same name differ: lines 209–214.
  - Verification: merge.py proves that when multiple inputs being merged expose the same coordinate name but with different underlying pd.Index objects, a MergeError is raised with essentially the message in the bug report. This matches the user's MergeError.

Conclusion from verification (methods): The draft's claims about which methods are involved and what they do are accurate. The exact code lines confirm that to_unstacked_dataset selects per-level slices and returns Dataset(data_dict) (without dropping the stacked coordinate), that to_stacked_array creates and assigns the stacked MultiIndex coordinate, and that merge/merge_collected/unique_variable will raise MergeError when the same-named coordinates/indexes differ between objects to be combined.

2) RootCause verification

The draft’s RootCause had two main points. I verified both against code:

- Draft RootCause item 1: DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset assumes selected DataArrays can be handed directly to Dataset(...), but they may still include the stacked MultiIndex coordinate (the coordinate named by dim), and those coordinate Index objects differ between per-variable slices, causing Dataset.__init__/merge to raise MergeError.
  - Verification: Code shows to_unstacked_dataset indeed selects and squeezes and then immediately calls Dataset(data_dict) (dataarray.py lines 1961–1967). It does not drop the stacked coordinate named `dim`. merge.py demonstrates that Dataset construction (merge_collected / unique_variable) validates / compares index objects and raises MergeError when they differ. Therefore this claim is supported by code.

- Draft RootCause item 2: The stacked coordinate is created by Dataset.to_stacked_array as an IndexVariable assigned to the new stacked dimension name (data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx)); keeping that stacked coordinate on the per-variable DataArrays preserves the MultiIndex on each and leads to conflicts.
  - Verification: Dataset.to_stacked_array assigns the IndexVariable to the DataArray (dataset.py line 3483). Because that coordinate is attached to the stacked DataArray, selections over MultiIndex levels (sel on level name) result in DataArrays that may still have the stacked coordinate present as a coordinate (the code in to_unstacked_dataset does not remove it). merge.py then detects conflicting index objects. This confirms the draft's second point.

I therefore confirm both draft root-cause points are valid and supported by the code; they share a single root cause pattern: the stacked MultiIndex coordinate is left on the per-variable slices handed to Dataset construction, provoking coordinate/index conflicts at merge time.

3) Claims in the draft not supported by code

- I found no contradicting claims in the draft. The draft’s statement that this is particularly exposed for single-dim variables (the MCVE) is supported: in the MCVE the stacked MultiIndex has only the variable level (no other stacking dims), and the per-variable selections land in different Index objects for the stacked coordinate, so the merge fails. The code path and merge behavior confirm this.

4) Missing important findings from the investigation analysis

- The investigation had the same two findings; I confirmed both are present and equally important. No other secondary findings sharing the same root-cause pattern were uncovered by the investigation that were omitted from the draft: the problematic pattern is specifically the stacked coordinate persisting on per-variable slices in to_unstacked_dataset. I also reviewed related codepaths (Dataset.unstack/_unstack_once and Dataset.to_stacked_array) and confirmed they do not themselves remove the stacked coordinate on the per-variable side; the correct place to address the conflict is in the unstack-to-dataset conversion.

5) Completeness check — code paths and impacted locations

- Where the same assumption (that selected/squeezed slices can be directly combined without dropping the stacked coordinate) appears:
  - DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (xarray/core/dataarray.py) — the code in question.
  - The stacked coordinate originates in Dataset.to_stacked_array (xarray/core/dataset.py), which assigns IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx) to the stacked DataArray.
  - The merge logic that raises the error is in xarray/core/merge.py (unique_variable and merge_collected).
- I checked Dataset.unstack/_unstack_once (dataset.py lines ~3490–3516). That logic is a different codepath (unstacking a Dataset via its own .unstack) and is not the immediate cause of this specific MergeError; the failure occurs during the Dataset construction from a mapping created by to_unstacked_dataset.
- There are no other to_unstacked_dataset implementations elsewhere; this is the unique entry point for the DataArray API that creates Dataset from a stacked DataArray in this manner.

6) Field discipline check (validate and adjust fields)

- Description should state only the bug. The draft’s Description was slightly verbose and mixed high-level behavior. I will rewrite it concisely.
- RootCause must list the incorrect assumptions and exact locations. I will keep both findings, list their locations, and ensure equal weight.
- ExpectedBehavior and ObservedBehavior should be strictly user-visible behavior (no implementation steps). I will rewrite both to be concise user-visible statements.
- StepsToReproduce are already concise and appropriate as a short MCVE; I will keep them.

7) Verification of additional locations (search)
- I searched for other occurrences of to_unstacked_dataset and to_stacked_array: there is exactly one implementation of DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (dataarray.py) and one Dataset.to_stacked_array (dataset.py) in core. The merge logic is centralized in merge.py.

Summary of verification
- The draft report’s diagnostic claims are accurate and supported by code. The root cause is correctly identified: the stacked MultiIndex coordinate (new_dim) created by Dataset.to_stacked_array remains attached to per-variable slices produced inside DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset and conflicts when Dataset(data_dict) attempts to merge coordinates/indexes. The MergeError originates from merge.py when comparing indexes/variables with the same name.

Phase 2 — Revised report JSON

Below is the revised bug report (diagnosis-only), a short list of edits I made to the draft, and evidence references (files/line ranges) from the code that support the diagnosis.

bug_report.Title: to_unstacked_dataset fails for variables with single non-sample dimension (MergeError on stacked coordinate)
bug_report.Description: Calling DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset on a DataArray produced by Dataset.to_stacked_array raises MergeError when the dataset's variables are single-dimension (i.e. sample_dims covers all variable dims). The error occurs when constructing a Dataset from the per-variable slices produced by the stacked DataArray.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (xarray/core/dataarray.py) constructs the dict of variables by selecting and squeezing per-variable slices and then passing that mapping directly to Dataset(...):
   - it does: for k in variables: data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True) (dataarray.py lines ~1961–1964). This code leaves the stacked coordinate (the MultiIndex coordinate named by the stacked-dimension argument `dim`) attached to each per-variable DataArray.

2) Dataset.to_stacked_array (xarray/core/dataset.py) creates the stacked MultiIndex and assigns it as an IndexVariable on the stacked DataArray (data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx), dataset.py line ~3483). Because that stacked coordinate remains present on per-variable selections, Dataset.__init__/merge (xarray/core/merge.py) attempts to merge those same-named coordinates across inputs and raises MergeError when their underlying Index objects differ (merge.py raises MergeError for conflicting variable/index values, e.g. unique_variable and merge_collected at lines ~142–145 and ~209–214).

Both findings share the same root-cause pattern: the stacked MultiIndex coordinate (the coordinate with name equal to the stacked-dimension argument) persists on the per-variable DataArrays that are fed into Dataset(...) and the differing Index objects for that stacked coordinate trigger merge-time conflicts. This is exposed in the MCVE where variables are single-dimension (stacked MultiIndex has only the variable level).
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Calling to_unstacked_dataset on a DataArray previously created by Dataset.to_stacked_array should return a Dataset whose data variables match the original dataset (a roundtrip). For the MCVE, unstacked should be a Dataset with data variables 'a' and 'b' each with dimension 'x' and no MergeError.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: to_unstacked_dataset raises MergeError: "conflicting values for variable 'y' on objects to be combined..." because the stacked MultiIndex coordinate named 'y' remains attached to each per-variable DataArray and their coordinate/index objects differ, leading the Dataset merge checks to fail.
